description: UE5 interfaces: UInterface/IInterface, Blueprint Interfaces, Execute_ calls, ImplementsInterface, and cross-class communication patterns

entries.title: Interfaces Overview — Why and When
entries.description: Interfaces define a contract (set of functions) that any class can implement regardless of inheritance. They enable polymorphic communication between unrelated classes — the caller doesn't need to know the concrete type.
entries.whatItIs: A UE5 Interface is a pair of classes (UInterface + IInterface) that declares function signatures without implementation. Any UObject-derived class can implement the interface and provide its own logic, enabling the caller to invoke functions on any actor without knowing its concrete type.
whenToUse: When multiple unrelated classes need the same behavior — e.g., doors, switches, NPCs, items all need an Interact() function
When you want loose coupling — the player calls Interact() without knowing if it's a door, chest, or NPC
When you need polymorphism without forcing a shared base class — interfaces work across any class hierarchy
When building modular systems: Damageable, Interactable, Saveable, Targetable interfaces that any actor can opt into
When Blueprint designers need to add behavior to actors without C++ changes
whenNotToUse: When only one class will ever implement the behavior — just put the function on that class directly
When you need shared state or data — interfaces only define functions, not variables (use a Component for shared data + behavior)
When a simple delegate/event would suffice for one-to-many communication
When all classes already share a common base class that can hold the function
commonMistakes: Implementing functions on UInterface instead of IInterface — UInterface is for reflection only, IInterface holds the actual function declarations
Forgetting to add the interface to the class declaration (public IMyInterface) — the class compiles but ImplementsInterface returns false
Using Cast<IMyInterface> instead of the correct pattern — use Cast<UMyInterface> or query with ImplementsInterface + Execute_ calls
Not understanding the difference between BlueprintImplementableEvent and BlueprintNativeEvent in interfaces
Trying to add UPROPERTY variables to an interface — interfaces can only declare functions, not data
keyPoints: Interfaces enable calling functions on any actor without knowing its class
Two-class pattern: UMyInterface (reflection) + IMyInterface (function declarations)
Any class can implement multiple interfaces — not limited to single inheritance
Check if an actor implements it: Actor->GetClass()->ImplementsInterface(UMyInterface::StaticClass())
Call interface functions: IMyInterface::Execute_FuncName(Actor, Params) for BlueprintNativeEvent/BlueprintImplementableEvent
Or cast: if (IMyInterface* Impl = Cast<IMyInterface>(Actor)) Impl->FuncName()
Blueprint Interfaces: created in editor, no C++ needed. Good for designer-driven systems.

entries.title: C++ Interface — Full Implementation Pattern
entries.description: The complete pattern for declaring a C++ interface, implementing it on an actor, and calling it from other code. This is the most common interface workflow in UE5 C++ projects.
entries.whatItIs: The full C++ workflow for UE5 interfaces: declare with UInterface/IInterface pair, implement on any class by inheriting IMyInterface, and call via Execute_ static functions or direct casting. BlueprintNativeEvent allows C++ defaults with Blueprint overrides.
whenToUse: When you need C++ performance and type safety for the interface functions
When the interface has a default C++ implementation that Blueprints can optionally override (BlueprintNativeEvent)
When you want both C++ and Blueprint classes to implement the same interface
When building core gameplay systems that need compile-time checking
whenNotToUse: When only Blueprint classes will implement the interface — use a Blueprint Interface instead (simpler)
When you need a quick prototype without touching C++ code
When the interface function signature changes frequently during development — Blueprint interfaces are faster to iterate
commonMistakes: Forgetting _Implementation suffix on BlueprintNativeEvent functions — the C++ body goes in FuncName_Implementation(), not FuncName()
Calling the function directly (Actor->Interact()) instead of using IInteractable::Execute_Interact(Actor) for BlueprintNativeEvent — direct calls skip Blueprint overrides
Not adding UFUNCTION() to interface functions that need Blueprint access — without it, Blueprints can't see or override the function
Implementing the interface but forgetting to override the virtual function — compiles but crashes or does nothing at runtime
Using ImplementsInterface check but then using a raw C++ cast instead of Execute_ — if the implementer is a Blueprint, the C++ cast fails
keyPoints: BlueprintNativeEvent: C++ provides default, Blueprint can override. Body in FuncName_Implementation().
BlueprintImplementableEvent: no C++ body, Blueprint MUST implement it.
BlueprintCallable: function is callable from Blueprint but implemented in C++ only.
Execute_FuncName(Object, Params): static function that correctly dispatches to C++ or Blueprint implementation.
Implement by inheriting: class AMyDoor : public AActor, public IInteractable
Check at runtime: Actor->Implements<UInteractable>() or Actor->GetClass()->ImplementsInterface()
Multiple interfaces: class AMyActor : public AActor, public IInteractable, public IDamageable

entries.title: Blueprint Interface — No C++ Required
entries.description: Blueprint Interfaces are created entirely in the editor. They define functions that any Blueprint can implement, enabling cross-Blueprint communication without C++ and without knowing the other Blueprint's class.
entries.whatItIs: A Blueprint Interface is an editor-only asset that declares function signatures (with optional inputs/outputs) that any Blueprint class can implement. Unlike C++ interfaces, they require zero code — everything is done visually in the editor.
whenToUse: When designers need to define and implement interfaces without C++ involvement
Rapid prototyping of interaction systems, damage systems, or any cross-actor communication
When all implementing classes are Blueprints (no C++ classes need to implement it)
Simple projects or game jams where speed matters more than C++ performance
whenNotToUse: When you need a C++ default implementation — use a C++ BlueprintNativeEvent interface instead
When interface functions are performance-critical (called hundreds of times per frame) — C++ interfaces are faster
When you need compile-time type checking in C++ code that calls the interface
commonMistakes: Sending a message to an actor that doesn't implement the interface and expecting an error — it silently does nothing, which can be hard to debug
Not implementing all functions from the interface in the Blueprint — unimplemented functions silently return default values
Confusing 'Add Interface' in Class Settings with 'Implement Interface' — you must do both: add the interface AND implement its functions in the event graph
Returning values from interface functions without setting the output pins in the implementation — returns zero/empty/false by default
Creating a new Blueprint Interface for every actor-to-actor call instead of reusing general-purpose interfaces
keyPoints: Create: Content Browser > Blueprint > Blueprint Interface
Define functions in the interface editor — set inputs, outputs, and names
Implement: open Blueprint > Class Settings > Interfaces > Add. Then implement each function in the EventGraph.
Call: use 'Send Message' node or 'Interface Call' to call functions on any actor — no cast needed
Does Implement Interface: Blueprint node to check if an actor implements it before calling
Return values: add output pins to interface functions. They work as function calls with returns.
One Blueprint can implement multiple interfaces simultaneously

entries.title: Common Interface Patterns — Interactable, Damageable, Saveable
entries.description: Real-world interface patterns used in UE5 projects. These three interfaces (Interactable, Damageable, Saveable) demonstrate how interfaces solve common gameplay architecture problems.
entries.whatItIs: A collection of battle-tested interface patterns that appear in most UE5 projects. Each one decouples a gameplay system (interaction, damage, saving) from specific actor types, allowing any actor to participate by implementing the interface.
whenToUse: When building an interaction system that works with doors, NPCs, items, vehicles, and any future interactable type
When building a damage system where different actors react differently to damage (characters lose HP, barrels explode, walls crack)
When building a save system that needs to ask each actor what data it wants to save and restore
When you want to add a new actor type to an existing system without modifying any existing code
whenNotToUse: For one-off interactions between two specific classes — a direct function call or event is simpler
When using GAS for damage — GAS has its own targeting and effect application that replaces a Damageable interface
When the interface would have only one implementer — premature abstraction
commonMistakes: Making interfaces too granular (one per function) instead of grouping related functions — IInteractable with Interact() + GetInteractText() + CanInteract() is better than three separate interfaces
Making interfaces too broad (one god-interface with 20 functions) — split by domain: IInteractable, IDamageable, ISaveable
Not providing a CanDoX() check function alongside DoX() — callers need to know if the action is valid before performing it
Duplicating interface logic across implementers instead of using a shared Component — if 10 classes have the same Interact logic, put it in a UInteractionComponent
Not considering that interfaces work on Components too — you can implement IInteractable on a UActorComponent, not just Actors
keyPoints: IInteractable: Interact(Caller), GetInteractText(), CanInteract(Caller) — used by player interaction traces
IDamageable: ApplyDamage(Amount, Type, Instigator), GetHealth(), IsAlive() — alternative to UE5's built-in damage if you need more control
ISaveable: GetSaveData() -> FSaveStruct, LoadSaveData(FSaveStruct) — each actor controls its own serialization
ITargetable: CanBeTargeted(Targeter), GetTargetLocation(), GetTargetPriority() — for AI and lock-on systems
ITeamMember: GetTeamId(), GetTeamAttitude(Other) — for friend/foe identification
Pattern: check CanX() → do X() → react to result. Always provide a validation function.
